chunks.title: Bix The Big 40 Energy Gel
chunks.content: Bix The Big 40 Energy Gel: This gel features an optimal 1:0.8 glucose-to-fructose ratio to maximize absorption, increase carbohydrate utilization, and decrease stomach distress. It is vegan, uses natural flavors and colors, is manufactured in a UK GMP-certified facility, and is Informed Sport batch tested. Engineered to deliver double the carbs and sodium of typical market gels, it simplifies fueling logistics for endurance athletes. Its light, fluid consistency reduces stickiness and makes it easy to ingest, while its "stay-on" tab packaging keeps the torn tip attached to prevent trail litter. The caffeinated variant is noted for being highly effective while remaining remarkably gentle on the stomach.

chunks.title: GU Original Energy Gel
chunks.content: GU Original Energy Gel: Uses a dual-source carbohydrate blend of maltodextrin and fructose. The maltodextrin converts quickly to energy molecules within minutes, while the fructose absorbs more slowly to provide a steady stream of energy, preventing the insulin spikes and subsequent sugar crashes associated with simple sugars. Formulated with a BCAA blend (L-Leucine, L-Valine, and L-Isoleucine) to help protect working muscles from breakdown, aid in energy production, and prevent mental fatigue during exertion. Contains sodium to maintain fluid balance and aid carbohydrate absorption. Contains 450mg of branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs).

chunks.title: GU Roctane Energy Gel
chunks.content: GU Roctane Energy Gel: Specifically engineered for long-duration and high-intensity activities requiring additional nutritional support. It delivers 3x the amino acids and 2 to 3x the electrolytes of GU's Original Gels. It uniquely includes the amino acids taurine (to help maintain heart contractility and improve cardiac output during long exercise sessions) and beta-alanine (to help buffer muscle acidity during intense efforts). Contains an amped-up BCAA/amino acid blend of 1,425mg per packet. The Lemonade flavor contains the highest sodium content out of any GU Energy Gel.

chunks.title: GU Liquid Energy Gel
chunks.content: GU Liquid Energy Gel: Affectionately called "The GU You Can Gulp," it features a thin, refreshingly light, and smooth liquid consistency designed for quick, easy intake on the go without the thickness of a traditional gel. Because of its higher water content, it can be frozen into a slushie for hot training days. Uses a dual-source carbohydrate system to provide separate "express lanes" for absorption, maximizing fuel delivery without stomach upset. It includes BCAAs to delay the onset of physical fatigue, decrease muscle breakdown, and jumpstart protein synthesis. Contains 450mg of amino acids per serving.

Designed with low sweetening power for a mild finish, making it easy to swallow when the heart rate is redlining. Formulated with eco-friendly ingredients like agave powder and tapioca starch, which use less water and emit less CO2 than conventional sugars. Certified by Informed Sport to be free of banned substances.

chunks.title: Näak Näak Boost™ Drink Mix 60 Neutral
chunks.content: Näak Näak Boost™ Drink Mix 60 Neutral: Features a 2:1 glucose-to-fructose ratio employing dual carbohydrate absorption pathways to maximize energy absorption without overloading a single pathway or causing gut distress. Blends Tapioca Maltodextrin (a complex carb that breaks down into glucose for prolonged energy release) with Isomaltulose (a slow-digesting, low-glycemic sugar derived from beets) to ensure stable energy output with no sugar crashes. Designed for a water-like consistency that glides easily in the mouth with exceptionally low sweetness. Formulated with 100% natural, allergen-free ingredients. Informed Sport certified.

chunks.title: Näak Näak Ultra Energy™ Gel | Salted Maple
chunks.content: Näak Näak Ultra Energy™ Gel | Salted Maple: Specifically engineered for ultra-endurance efforts where simple carbohydrate ingestion alone is insufficient. Uses a precise 1:1 ratio of short (simple) and complex carbohydrates to provide an immediate energy spike without the subsequent "sugar crash," alongside a 3:1 glucose-to-fructose ratio to optimize intestinal sugar absorption. Formulated with plant-based proteins and branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs: Leucine, Isoleucine, Valine) to reduce muscle fatigue and optimize cellular glycogen storage. The low glycemic load and smooth, semi-liquid texture make it easy to consume at speed and highly digestible under physical stress. Highly sustainable: made using 37% upcycled maple syrup.

chunks.title: PF Carb Only Drink Mix
chunks.content: PF Carb Only Drink Mix: Developed in collaboration with pro cycling team Lotto Dstny for the Tour de France. It contains zero sodium, giving athletes the flexibility to scale their carbohydrate intake up to 120g/hr from their bottles while independently managing their sodium needs using electrolyte capsules or tablets. It features a clean, light, and neutral taste to completely prevent flavor fatigue and gastrointestinal issues over long-duration events.

chunks.title: Science in Sport (SiS) Beta Fuel Dual Source Energy Drink (BETA FUEL 80 Sachet)
chunks.content: Science in Sport (SiS) Beta Fuel Dual Source Energy Drink (BETA FUEL 80 Sachet): Formulated as an all-in-one convenient isotonic fueling solution for endurance athletes whose events last more than two hours. It utilizes an optimized 1:0.8 maltodextrin-to-fructose ratio (replacing the traditional 2:1 ratio) to increase the efficiency of ingested carbohydrate oxidation from 62% to 74%. This specific ratio enhances exogenous carbohydrate oxidation by 10% to 17% and increases mean power output during maximal sprint efforts by 3%. Crucially, it significantly reduces self-reported symptoms of stomach fullness and nausea.

chunks.title: Carbohydrate Intake Targets and Multiple Transportable Carbohydrates
chunks.content: Carbohydrates are the primary fuel burned during high-intensity endurance racing [1-3]. Because endogenous glycogen stores in the liver (~100g) and skeletal muscles (~500g) are finite and typically become depleted after 90 to 120 minutes of hard activity, exogenous carbohydrate intake is necessary to sustain performance [3-7]. Scientific recommendations for hourly carbohydrate intake are structured around exercise duration, intensity, and athletic tier: Under 1 Hour: Starting well-fueled generally requires no carbohydrate intake [8, 9]. For all-out efforts lasting 45 to 60 minutes, a carbohydrate mouth rinse may offer neurological and performance benefits [9, 10]. 1 to 2 Hours: Athletes should aim for 30g to 60g of simple carbohydrates per hour, scaling toward the upper limit as intensity increases [8, 10, 11]. 2+ Hours (Very Prolonged Events): Athletes should target 60g to 90g of carbohydrates per hour to maximize oxidation rates, maintain blood glucose, and delay fatigue [8, 10, 12-14]. The High-Carb Frontier (≥100g/h): Trained endurance and elite athletes frequently target 100g to 120g+ per hour [15-17]. A benchmark study by Viribay et al. (2020) demonstrated that 7 of 9 trained runners successfully tolerated 120g/h during a mountain marathon, showing reduced muscle damage markers and lower internal load [10, 17, 18]. Stellingwerff and Cox (2014) also advised a broad target range of 40g/h to 110g/h for events exceeding 2 hours [19]. For any intake exceeding 60g/h, athletes must utilize multiple transportable carbohydrates (typically a glucose-fructose or maltodextrin-fructose blend) to avoid saturating the gut's primary SGLT1 glucose transporters [35-37]. Utilizing a 2:1 ratio of glucose-to-fructose unlocks separate absorption pathways to increase total carbohydrate delivery [36, 38-40]. An advanced 1:0.8 ratio of maltodextrin to fructose (found in Science in Sport Beta Fuel or Maurten) is proven to be even more efficient, raising whole-body carbohydrate oxidation efficiency from 62% to 74% compared to a 2:1 ratio [41, 42]. It increases exogenous oxidation by 10% to 17% and significantly reduces self-reported symptoms of stomach fullness and nausea [41, 42].

chunks.title: The Physiology and Benefits of Gut Training
chunks.content: The gastrointestinal tract is highly plastic and acts as the ultimate rate-limiting bottleneck for exogenous carbohydrate delivery [43, 44]. Pushing high carb numbers without prior adaptation regularly induces severe gastrointestinal symptoms (bloating, sloshing, diarrhea, and vomiting) due to malabsorption and delayed gastric emptying under physical stress [45-49]. Physiological Adaptations of Gut Training: 1. Reduces Malabsorption and GI Distress: Exposing the gut to high carbohydrate volumes during exercise over a short 2-week period can reduce gastrointestinal symptoms and malabsorption by as much as half (50%) [50, 51]. 2. Up-regulates Intestinal Sugar Transporters: Chronically supplementing your daily training diet with high carbohydrate availability (such as 1.5g of carbohydrate per kg of body weight for every hour of exercise performed daily over 28 days) significantly increases exogenous carbohydrate oxidation rates, likely by up-regulating the density and activity of SGLT1 (glucose) and GLUT5 (fructose) transporters in the small intestine [44, 52]. 3. Spares Glycogen and Prevents "Bonking": Consuming high carbohydrate volumes at race intensity allows for stable blood glucose availability and spares endogenous muscle and liver glycogen stores [39, 53]. Specifically, maintaining an intake near 100g/h has been shown to successfully preserve liver glycogen [53]. 4. Improves Fluid Tolerance: Gut training must also build tolerance to fluid volume [54]. Highly concentrated carbohydrate drinks increase the osmolality of the gut; training the stomach to handle both high carbohydrate densities and necessary fluid volumes (e.g., 500ml to 1,000ml per hour to match sweat rates) prevents fluid from "sloshing" around and causing nausea [45, 54, 55].

chunks.title: A Step-by-Step Protocol for Building Carbohydrate Tolerance
chunks.content: Building carbohydrate tolerance requires a systematic, progressive program executed over several weeks. 1. Establish the Daily Diet Foundation: Eat adequate carbohydrates daily: Do not train on a chronic low-carbohydrate or ketogenic (LCHF) diet if you intend to race on carbohydrates [56, 57]. A lack of daily carbohydrates down-regulates your gut microbiome and transporter pathways, increasing your risk of severe GI distress on race day [58]. 2. Timeline and Session Selection: Begin your structured gut training plan 6 to 8 weeks before your target race [59]. Choose 1, preferably 2, training sessions per week to practice your fueling [59, 60]. These sessions should be your longest and hardest workouts that closely mimic race-day intensity and environmental conditions [59]. 3. The Progressive Loading Protocol: Begin fueling at an hourly rate you are 100% confident will not cause GI distress [59]. Progressively increase your intake by ~10g/h every 1 to 2 weeks [60, 61]. Hold at the new dose for at least 1 to 2 weeks until your gut feels entirely comfortable and symptom-free before stepping up again [59, 60]. Aim to train at a carbohydrate rate that exceeds your race-day target by at least 20% [53]. 4. In-Session Execution and Logistics: Start consuming carbohydrates 15 to 20 minutes into your training run [59]. Aim to consume small, digestible doses of 20g to 30g every 15 to 20 minutes [64] or take a quarter of a jumbo gel pouch every 15 minutes [65]. You must drink plain water alongside gels (e.g., 300ml to 600ml per hour) or use pre-mixed electrolyte drinks to support gastric emptying and carbohydrate transport [45, 59, 67, 68]. Practise only with the exact sports nutrition brands, flavors, and formats you intend to use on race day [59, 60]. For sessions under 2 to 3 hours or very high intensities, rely almost exclusively on liquid carbohydrate drink mixes and highly digestible gels [59, 69]. As training duration extends and intensity drops, selectively introduce solid options [59, 69]. Record the exact grams of carbohydrates consumed per hour, formats used, fluid volumes, and subjective ratings of energy and gut comfort [59].

chunks.title: Sodium Intake Targets and Strategies
chunks.content: Sweat sodium concentration is genetically determined and highly stable, yet varies drastically among individuals from 200 mg to over 2,000 mg per liter, with an average athlete losing about 991 mg/L. Government guidelines recommend a daily intake limit of 2,300 mg, which a heavily sweating athlete can lose in just one hour of exercise. Rather than absolute hourly targets, sports science suggests focusing on the relative sodium concentration in drinks (mg/L) to prevent GI issues and excessive thirst: ~500 mg/L for low-sweat/cool weather, ~1,000 mg/L as a baseline, and ~1,500 mg/L for heavy sweaters or hot climates. General hourly sodium supplementation during exercise ranges from 300 mg to 800 mg, with some brands recommending 200 mg to 600 mg. When using table salt (39% sodium), approximately 3g is needed for ~1,170 mg of sodium. A preloading protocol involves drinking ~500 ml of a high-sodium electrolyte drink the night before a race, and another ~500 ml on race morning, finishing 45-60 minutes prior to allow absorption and urination. A decoupled fueling strategy, separating carbohydrate intake from hydration (plain water and electrolyte capsules), enables dynamic adjustment of fluid and sodium based on weather and sweat rates without impacting calorie math.

chunks.title: Fluid Management and Environmental Factors
chunks.content: Sweat rate is highly individual, influenced by temperature, humidity, work rate, clothing, genetics, and heat acclimation. While 100% sweat replacement is unnecessary and can cause issues like hyponatremia, body mass losses exceeding 2% impair performance, reduce blood volume, and elevate core temperature and heart rate. In temperate conditions (~8–15°C), 300-600 ml of fluid per hour is a suitable target. For heat adaptation training, 50–65 kg individuals should target 1–2 L/h sweat rates, and 65 kg+ individuals 1.5–2.5+ L/h. Fluid needs scale with weather: cold weather may require only ~100 ml/hour, while hot weather can demand ~1.1 L/hour or more, as seen in elite athletes. High humidity severely hinders sweat evaporation, compromising cooling, elevating core temperatures, and increasing sweat rates, which also redirects blood flow from the gut. To counter heat-induced GI distress, small, frequent carbohydrate doses (every 10–20 minutes) are better than large hourly boluses. Proactive cooling tactics like ice bandanas or dousing with cold water mitigate thermal strain and reduce dehydration risk by modulating sweat rate.

chunks.title: Hydration, Sodium, and Cramping
chunks.content: A landmark 30-year study analyzing over 49,000 Kona triathletes demonstrated a strong relationship between dehydration and cramping, showing that cramping athletes lost significantly more body mass than non-cramping athletes (-1.95 kg vs. -1.49 kg). The same Kona study showed that cramping and non-cramping athletes had identical blood sodium levels at the point of treatment. However, blood sodium levels are not a proxy for total body sodium status. The body tightly regulates blood sodium concentration to preserve life; during heavy sweat losses, it draws sodium from other compartments—including the interstitial fluid surrounding the muscles—to keep blood levels stable. Consequently, local muscle-level dehydration and sodium depletion can be severe even when a blood test appears completely normal. In a classic 1930s study by Dr. R.A. McCance, subjects subjected to a low-salt diet combined with sweating hot baths experienced severe muscle cramps, weakness, and exertion distress. Crucially, reintroducing sodium led to a dramatic recovery and the immediate cessation of cramping within 15 minutes of ingestion.

chunks.title: Pre-Race Nutrition & Hydration
chunks.content: An effective pre-race strategy focuses on saturating internal (endogenous) carbohydrate stores in the muscles and liver while establishing a strong hydration and electrolyte buffer. Muscle glycogen stores are maximized by consuming 8 to 12 grams of carbohydrate per kilogram of body weight per day for the 24 to 48 hours (1 to 2 days) leading up to race day. To hit these high numbers without overwhelming the digestive system, athletes must increase simple carbohydrates while actively reducing fat and fiber intake to prevent bloating and GI distress. Lean liquid carbs (drink mixes, juices, gels, and sweets) are highly effective tools to pack in calories without adding physical volume to meals. Glycogen is stored alongside water; each gram of glycogen binds with at least 3 grams of water. This frequently causes a noticeable pre-race weight gain, which is a normal indicator that your energy reserves are fully stocked. For example, Peter Tierney (81 kg) consumed 749g of carbs (9.2 g/kg) on RD-2 and 858g of carbs (10.6 g/kg) on RD-1, and Rachel Entrekin hit ~7.7 g/kg the day before her Cocodona 250 victory. Overnight, liver glycogen levels drop significantly. To top them up, athletes should consume 1 to 4 grams of carbohydrates per kilogram of body mass within 1 to 4 hours before the starting gun. Megan Hill had a robust pre-race breakfast of two bagels and orange juice, hitting ~1.8 g/kg, while Brad Williams took in ~1.2 g/kg. Conversely, Tom Caldecourt fell below guidelines by eating only ~0.8 g/kg, likely leaving him with sub-optimal liver glycogen reserves. Taking a 30g carbohydrate "hit" in the final 15 to 20 minutes before the race elevates circulating blood glucose levels and "spares" stored liver and muscle glycogen for the latter portions of the event. To expand blood plasma volume and establish a fluid safety reservoir, athletes should drink ~500ml (16 oz) of a high-sodium electrolyte drink the night before, and another ~500ml (16 oz) of the same drink on race morning, finishing it 45 to 60 minutes before the start to allow the body to absorb the fluid and excrete any excess.

chunks.title: Real Food vs. Gels During Long Races
chunks.content: Endurance athletes must decide how to package their carbohydrate intake, as both gels/drinks and real foods have distinct physiological impacts and practical trade-offs. Gels and drink mixes are overwhelmingly preferred by elite-level athletes under race stress. For example, Eli Hemming took 73% of his carbs from gels and 21% from drink mixes during his 7th-place finish at CCC by UTMB. Formulated sports nutrition delivers consistent, fast-absorbing carbohydrates (typically using maltodextrin/glucose and fructose) that require minimal digestive energy under physical stress. Newer hydrogel-based products encapsulate carbohydrates, allowing them to bypass breakdown in the stomach acid for fast absorption in the intestine, reducing GI distress. Other brands use agar-agar to create a textured gel that encapsulates sugars to mitigate sweet taste fatigue and throat burn. Digestion is sport-specific; adventure racer Dougal Allan notes that the mechanical bouncing of running limits his gut tolerance to 70–90g/h, whereas the stable seated posture of kayaking allows him to comfortably tolerate 120g/h+ using gels and liquid mixes. During ultra-endurance events lasting 6+ hours, the palate often rejects cloying sweetness. Introducing savory real foods (such as salted mashed potatoes, broth, white rice, oatmeal, or pretzels) provides a welcome change of texture and keeps athletes eating. However, real foods carry significantly higher digestive demands because they contain a mix of macronutrients. Consuming fat and fiber during intensive exercise is highly problematic; for example, coach Paul Booth warns that consuming fats (like avocados or olive oil) slows gastric emptying and can easily trigger vomiting under high-intensity race stress.

chunks.title: Common Race Fueling Mistakes
chunks.content: Even experienced endurance athletes frequently fall victim to severe fueling and hydration mistakes. Over-fueling or forcing extreme intakes (120-140g/h) without gut training overloads intestinal transporters, causing gastric backup, nausea, or projectile vomiting. Elite runner Tom Booth, for instance, DNF'ed at UTMB trying to force high intakes, but later succeeded by dialing his target back to a stomach-friendly range. Conversely, chronic under-fueling is prevalent, with many amateur athletes underestimating carbohydrate costs. A 2012 study showed 73% of marathon runners failed to reach even a modest recommendation of 30-60 grams of carbohydrate per hour. Athlete Mel Caldecourt, consuming only ~17g/h, hit 'the wall' at mile 19. Dehydration-induced gut shutdown occurs when athletes neglect plain water while consuming concentrated gels and electrolyte pills, increasing gut osmolality and hindering carbohydrate absorption. Dehydration also diverts blood from the gut to working muscles and skin, inducing inflammation, increasing gut permeability, and causing severe nausea. Dehydration exceeding a 2% body weight loss typically degrades performance; founder Andy Blow experienced debilitating muscle cramps due to a 3.1% body weight loss during a hot marathon. Relying on single-source carbs at high intakes (over 60g/h) saturates SGLT1 transporters, causing nausea. Lauren Hall suffered severe nausea from single-source gels at ~76g/h. Transitioning to dual-source carbohydrates (e.g., 2:1 glucose-to-fructose) utilizes separate absorption pathways, allowing higher intake with lower gut stress. Logistical failures and lack of spares are also common. Brad Williams missed a planned gel due to miscalculated aid station, suffering an energy dip. Hayden Hawks accidentally threw away his high-carb gel, dropping his hourly intake. Over-drinking on race morning to compensate for prior dehydration also frequently backfires, as Brad Williams experienced by overloading his bladder and forcing an unplanned stop.
